sources: Member States Assessed Contributions (via 0.2% Import Levy)
International Partners (EU, China, World Bank, Google Partnership)

2026.methodology: The 2026 projection reflects the $700,000,000 baseline identified in the February 2026 AU Summit reports. Despite a target of $1B for security, realized funding tracks closely to this $700M figure. Spending shift (45% for Security) is driven by 2026 priorities in Sudan, Somalia, and the Sahel. Income includes a notable $30M infrastructure grant from China and private-sector digital initiatives (Google AI Partnership). The 0.2% levy remains the primary internal mechanism, though only 17 member states are fully compliant as of Q1 2026.

2025.methodology: Data based on the AU 2025 budget resolution adopted in Accra (July 2024). The total of $608,248,415 consists of a regular budget of $555,319,415 and specialized funds. Operational budget ($167,045,485) is almost entirely funded by member states (98%), while the Programme budget ($388,273,929) remains heavily partner-dependent (77.5%). Peace support operations ($52,929,131) are currently 100% funded by international partners as per the 2025 fiscal framework.

sources: International Development Partners (EU, USA, China, Germany)

2024.methodology: Official 2024 budget data approved during the AU Summit. Total amount of 605,756,610 USD is split into Operational ($172.6M), Programme ($372.6M), and Peace Support ($60.5M). Member states are responsible for 100% of the operational budget, while international partners cover 77% of the programme budget and 100% of peace support operations as per the 2024 fiscal framework.
